definition: A virtual environment is an isolated, per-project Python installation with its own interpreter link and site-packages directory; venv is the standard-library tool that creates one, and virtualenv is the older third-party tool that inspired it.
layman: It's a clean, private sandbox for each project's packages, so installing something for one project never disturbs another or your system Python.
content:
## Virtual environments (venv & virtualenv)

Without isolation, every `pip install` lands in one shared location, so two projects needing different versions of the same library collide. A **virtual environment** gives each project its own packages.

### Create and activate (venv)

```bash
python -m venv .venv            # create an env in ./.venv
source .venv/bin/activate       # activate (macOS/Linux)
# .venv\Scripts\activate       # activate (Windows PowerShell/cmd)
python -m pip install openai    # installs into .venv only
deactivate                      # leave the env
```

When active, `python` and `pip` point at the env's copies; installs go to `.venv/lib/.../site-packages`, not the system Python.

### venv vs virtualenv

| | venv | virtualenv |
|--|------|-----------|
| Source | standard library (3.3+) | third-party (`pip install virtualenv`) |
| Speed/features | core subset | faster creation, more options, older-Python support |
| When to use | default for most projects | when you need its extra speed/features or legacy Pythons |

### Why this matters for Gen AI

Gen AI stacks pin specific versions (e.g. a particular `openai`, `langchain`, `torch`, or CUDA-bound build). A per-project env lets each app keep the exact versions it was tested against, makes setup reproducible for teammates and CI, and avoids polluting the system Python.
keyPoints: A virtual environment isolates a project's packages from other projects and system Python.
Create with python -m venv .venv; activate, install, then deactivate.
When active, python/pip resolve to the env's own copies and site-packages.
venv is the built-in standard tool; virtualenv is the faster, fuller third-party predecessor.
Isolation enables reproducible, conflict-free per-project dependencies.
